video_title: Andrew Feldman on Building a Chip 58x Larger Than Nvidia's
transcript: The demand for AI has outpaced everybody's expectation, everybody's [music] forecast. And so everybody's chasing. They're chasing chips or they're chasing memory or they're chasing data centers. Open AAI, we announced in January a huge partnership, one of the the biggest deals done in in [music] Silicon Valley history. We'll be doing more than 20 billion of hardware for [music] them over the next several years. I'm concerned that there are ways frequently for Nvidia to exercise market [music] strength. their ways for to use their balance sheet to limit competition. We have a chance for our children or the next generation not only to not die from cancer but to not know anybody who died from cancer. [music] >> Andrew Felvin, welcome to Sorcery. >> Well, thank you so much for having me. We are out here in Paris at Rays. >> Pretty okay, huh? >> It's pretty okay. I also heard you're one of the most popular people here. [laughter] >> That would be first in my life. So So it's nice to nice of you to say >> a big theme though is and we were we were talking about this just off camerara is hardware is cool. Semiconductors are cool. Everything is in fashion now. >> We are in fashion. We're in demand. We're we're hard to get. [laughter] It's uh it's a big change. I think what's happened is the demand for AI has sort of outpaced everybody's expectation, everybody's forecast. And so everybody's chasing uh they're chasing chips or they're chasing memory or they're chasing data centers. Um there's an opportunity to invent new things, different architectures. It's really a time of sort of a Cambrian explosion of of of ideas. That's really fun. So you were at Raise last year. I remember this because I think you had the biggest booth here. There was a fence around it. >> Yeah. >> And a lot of people What is the biggest difference between last year and this year? >> Oh, I I I think last year was their first year, right? And I I think uh there are more people here. There are more participants. So there more attendees. There more companies participating. I think people are participating in a bigger way. I think the dinner last night at the uh Versail >> secret location >> in a secret location called Versailles [laughter] was sort of awesome. You know, we we spend a lot of time in the digital world >> and the people we talk about AI to to go look at something that that illiterate masons made 400 years ago, right? Is and to sit in it and to enjoy the the grandeur is is extraordinary, right? This wasn't AI. This wasn't EDA design or CAD or these were guys with pen and paper communicating with, you know, masons and and and other tradesmen who couldn't read and they built something unbelievably beautiful and that was really fun. >> Mhm. And so you're going to be on stage this year. What are you mainly going to >> I'm on stage with uh Satchin from OpenAI. >> Um you know we we announced in January a huge partnership, one of the the biggest deals done in in Silicon Valley history. uh we'll be doing more than 20 billion dollars of of hardware uh with for them over the next several years. We'll talk a little bit about deploying hardware and fast AI and how how important fast uh inference is in the emerging sort of inference AI landscape. >> Inference is a hot topic. Everyone loves inference. >> Yeah. We we we make AI with training, right? And we use AI with inference. And so as the the the models and as the AI we made becomes useful, everybody wants to use it and and inference is the mechanism through which we use it. And so now we have smart smart AI and people want to use it and when they want to use it, they want to use it and they want to be fast. And that's sort of where we come in and where the fast is not by a little bit but by 20x and and so um everybody's using their AI. They're trying new things. They're deploying you know GPT or or cloud code or or or one of these super models. And um it's sort of an explosion of of of building of trying new things of a new way to work. It's pretty fun. So, we're nearly two months after your IPO, and there was one post that I thought was really cool. You posted that you had a very large chip on your shoulder [laughter] >> and it was quite literal and physical. [laughter] I I hoped that that, you know, now that we're running a public company, that that I I I wouldn't lose sort of the self-deprecating humor, [laughter] whatever, that that I enjoy the the sort of tone in in my social posts. And so, we uh put a giant chip, we put this sort of on my shoulder like this, and we built a harness for it. And [laughter] uh yeah, that was a fun one. Um uh I you know I I think that uh people assume that that that a as a CEO or uh of any size or an entrepreneur that that it's sort of always peaches and cream and it's just not the case. There's an enormous amount of hard work. There's sacrifice uh that you make and that your family makes. They see you less and it's not for a little bit. It's not like like a weekend or two weeks in a row you work hard. for years and and so sharing that a little bit is uh something I thought would would would be wellreceived. >> So what's been the biggest difference for you post IPO? >> Um the number of people who want something >> really. >> Yeah. Has exploded >> um of one form or another. And uh we get a little better at at at triaging those um between my my executive assistant and chief of staff and just if you get 80 emails a day of of different people asking you for something. That's not work. That that's just through this range of of people who who want something of one form or another to meet you, your time, for you to present uh for you to donate to their cause, for you to Whoa. Um, that was a little unexpected. >> Your last company you helped create a hundred millionaires. This company you have, I don't know, countless more. >> Maybe a thousand. >> Maybe future thousand, right? >> At IPO is more. It was about a thousand. >> Would that be investors and employees or >> employees? Really? >> Current and former. >> Okay. Wow. And so what lesson do you learn from that as a CEO? Like what is in your mind? >> I think a couple things. I think to to do the job you got love to build. >> I think uh making money is really great and making money for people you care about is really really great. >> And when you get a chance to >> deliver for people who bet on you, who who bet chunks of their career, right? Your investors, it's great to deliver for them. They bet on you, but they're diversified. They bet on you and 20 other companies. When someone bets five or seven years of their career in a career is 30 years, right? They're betting a sixth of their professional career. And when you get to deliver for them and and uh they get to to achieve the financial goals that that they wanted, that's a great feeling and what I'm proud of every day. >> It's a very selfless position on that. I mean it's really interesting because today I mean in the backdrop we are in a hyper super cycle whatever you want to call it of AI and so these companies that are coming up whether they're in semiconductors or they're in I don't know models or you know coding agents there's a lot of companies that are rising up really fast and hitting that billion dollar mark. Some are having liquidity events like OpenAI and Anthropic to come. Um, but others are are getting that paper markup and they're they're seeing that kind of wealth creation event. How do you maintain a healthy mindset around that? >> I think you you bring it you you you it's not a change, right? you um for the type of people I love working with, they they like building stuff and they like building hard stuff when it paid a little, when it was out of fashion, you know, when when when hardware was uncool, they were still building hardware because that's what they like to build. And now that it's in fashion, they like to build hardware. And they're sort of even killed about that that their passion is the building. And I I think in in Silicon Valley, which sort of is what I know, that um chasing money is not the path to money. That that the path to happiness is working on projects you like with colleagues that are interesting for for people with integrity. And if you do that, the money will come. But even more importantly, you work on things you like and you'll work with people you learn a little something from and you can teach a little something from. And if if that's the way you you said about pursuing your career, I think when things are really bad, you're on even keel. And when things are really good, you're on even kill because you you're enjoying what you're doing. >> This episode is brought to you by Brex, my favorite. You become what you spend on. And I refuse to spend my time on work that shouldn't exist. Expense reports, receipt chasing, and manual closes. The companies building what's next from Verscell, OpenAI, Anthropic, Granola, and Deepgram Graham all made the same call. They all run on Brex. Brex is the intelligent finance platform that combines cards, expenses, and banking into a single stack with Aentic Finance builtin. AI agents that handle expenses automatically, enforce policy before spend happens, and close your books in minutes. That's why Sorcery runs on Brex so I can spend time on building and not busy work. It's time to get Brex af. Learn more at brex.com/sourcy. That's br.coms ury. Bye. So looking forward, I'm sure this is also just a mark in your journey because you're a builder. So, what are you most looking forward to over the next couple months as we said across this year? >> Look, getting to an IPO is is not the the the end of a journey. It it's sort of a plateau. It's sort of the arrival at corporate adulthood. It is the achieving one one plateau so that you can climb others. And our opportunity has gotten bigger. We have more resources. we have uh we're better recognized uh we can reach more people um and we can sort of prosecute our vision and our our ambitions uh with more fuel. And so that that's what we're excited about every day, you know, building more chips, building more data centers, inventing technology that that moves the industry forward. Um that's what what drives us and gets us out of bed every day. I think I was listening to a Harry Stubbings episode that you did and talking about data centers and the demand for them and how we're actually not trying to meet future demand. We're actually just really behind. >> We are behind >> on where we are. So, could you explain where we are with the data center buildout, >> right? So, what happened is is data centers had historically moved at sort of the speed of real estate, right? somebody would decide to build a building and two years later after permits and they poured concrete and the building would go up and two years ago nobody cared about AI right two years ago we were at the beginning of this and the the AI explosions happened so quickly there's so much demand for compute we've outstripped the the demand for for compute for memory and where do these things go they go to these buildings and we they haven't made them fast enough and so people are chasing data centers around the around the world and that's what's happening and so um it is a a major limitation for for everybody right now. >> We just had Tony Kim on and we were talking about the new architecture of data centers. So how are you working with other companies whether it's through your buildouts creating the right stack and architecture? >> Sure. So you know the the interesting about you know data centers hadn't changed a lot in 20 years and a lot of the infrastructure in them hadn't changed. I mean the the generators that we use for backup have been unchanged for about 20 years. the type of batteries we use for backup, the uh the the chillers and the the the CDUs and the um and suddenly there's this sort of intense demand and and sort of people are looking to innovate and they're using fuel cells and guys are using jet engines like boom to to generate power for these data centers and the turbines are are are being rethought of and um and so there's sort of an innovative push in the data center. Now, for us, while we build this sort of super big chip, the chip that's like 58 times larger than any other chip, it goes in it goes in a a server. It goes in a a metal enclosure that's about the size of a fridge for a dorm room, little fridge. And we put a couple of those in a standard rack. And so, the the the physical requirements weren't very different from if you're going to deploy a competitor's product. Um what what Tonyy's really talking about is sort of these buildings were uh sort of unimproved for a generation and now they're part of a factory that that makes AI and people are trying to optimize every part of that factory. >> Yeah. And now they're also putting them in space. >> Yeah. Well, they're talking about putting them in space. [laughter] So I I think like a lot of technology, you talk about it for a long time before it happens. >> Do you have plans? >> Um, you know, I'm we we are really good for space because one of the hardest problems in space is getting all these little chips to talk to each other. >> And because we're a big chip, we don't have that that problem. Um, I don't think we're in danger in the near term of having a data center in space. Uh, I I think it's more than five years away. >> Five years. five years. >> Okay. >> And that's a long time in our world, >> right? I mean, three years ago, nobody was using AI, >> right? And [clears throat] so, let I I think we got a lot of work in building data centers on Earth before we actually have big production data centers in in space. >> I'd love to talk about how chip design has changed. >> Sure. co-design has become an important part of how do you create that kind of platform for the next three years because you're you're building for the next three years. So how do you think about that and how do you go about chip design? Well, I I I think historically you you made chips and you ran software on them and uh there wasn't surprisingly a a close interaction because there was a a layer called an operating system that that lived between the chip and the software. And so uh you know Intel and AMD made chips and people wrote software for the operating system or for the chip. AI has gotten so large and uh speed is so important that that what they're doing is they're thinking about sort of the design together that what changes could we make in software that would advantage the hardware or as we're designing the hardware what changes could we make that would make the software easier to run and so they're being designed sort of at the same time and like anything when you sort of design things together um the the advantages are enormous and and so this is something that's really taken shape right now. Um you know, one of the advantages of our relationship with Open AI is we we get to see exactly w where the the frontier is going and we get a chance to to roll that into our designs. One of the advantages Google has is the their TPU can be designed in collaboration with the the team building Gemini or the team of Deep Mind. And so they can they can inform their choices back and forth and and that's an enormously powerful thing that is surprisingly relatively new in our space. >> What do you think that the biggest misconception with that processes and the challenges are? Well, I I think that the misconception is that it's easy and all you need to do is get in a room and um it's a uh it's a very hard problem. Um you know, the the software guys think one way, the hardware guys think a slightly different way. Um you know, anything you do to make it easier to to to write the software makes it harder to do the hardware, >> right? And these are really hard tradeoffs. And so bringing them together um and means the these sort of compromises where it will be harder here to make it easier here. >> Mhm. >> And that means somebody's schedule's going to be impacted. Somebody's got to add resources. Um those discussions are enormously difficult. >> I'm really curious because I come from an outside perspective. You have an inside perspective. There's a lot of big deals that are being thrown around left and right. And I don't know what's actually under the headline. So when SpaceX comes out and they say they now have multi-billion dollar deals with Google and also with Reflection, like what does that actually mean? Like what are they selling them? It's >> tough to tell. No, it's tough to tell inside as well, >> really. >> Yeah. I I I think um there've been announced some sort of deals that didn't have teeth. um deals that that could have teeth later. >> Um I I I think um X had available capacity. And you got to ask why they had available capacity. They had available capacity because the Grock model wasn't used very much. So they had these GPUs that were sitting around and that's a bad idea. And so they sold a whole block of them or released a whole block of Ananthropic and they looked up and said, "Whoa, that's a pretty good idea, right? We had all these GPUs. Our model wasn't a success, but whoa, we can have a great business by sort of stepping into what is a constrained market. Very hard to get lots of of GPUs and and lease these." And then they looked around and said, "Well, what else can we who else can we lease to?" And and so that's how it started. Uh the specifics of those deals I'm not super familiar with. >> Are you concerned at all about the circular deals that are going on? >> Um I I'm concerned that that there are ways frequently for uh Nvidia to exercise market strength. >> Okay. >> Right. their ways for Nvidia to um to use their balance sheet to to to limit competition, right? That that if they invest in an in a NeoCloud, right? The Neocloud is less likely to use a non Nvidia chip. If they invest in a model builder, uh there's pressure not to use other people's chips. That's what I'm more more worried about. >> Yeah, this is happening in the token world with all the free tokens that are being offered to these startups. >> I That's exactly right. I I I I think these are uh these are drug pushers. Here, little girl, try a little bit. Just a little bit. And I I think uh what the startup should do is you know they should take it and then never be dependent and take some from AMD and come to us and see if we can get get you some as well and avoid dependence. That never ends well. >> If you're building what's next in AI, you need to know MongoDB, the database platform developers love and built for the agents you're running. MongoDB stores, searches, and reasons over your data in real time with vector search and embeddings from Voyage AI all in the same system. No separate pipelines, no stitching together 10 different tools. It's why 75% of the Fortune 100 and leading AI native startups run on MongoDB. Build and scale from your first user to billions of vectors. Go to mongodb.com/ai to learn more. That's mongodb.com/ai to learn more. Bye. Assembly AI is a voice AI infrastructure layer millions of developers build on. They build the industry's best speechtoext, voice agent, and speech understanding models that serve as critical infrastructure for companies like Granola, Hen, Ashby, and ClickUp. Their speechtoext models lead the industry in accuracy and quality. and their speech understanding models help you go beyond transcription by uncovering insights, identifying speakers, and highlighting key information from voice data. You can get started today at assemblyai.com/sourcy and get $50 of free credits to start building voice AI products. That's assemblyai.com y. I mean, this is actually a good transition into something that Karp said not too long ago on CNBC. He was pretty much talking about sovereign AI and how every company should own the full stack and so whether it's their data for their products and everything in between and models too because you don't want to sell that then the models will recreate it and then pull out a new Figma. >> Um how do you feel about sovereign AI and this like this shift over to owning the full stack? Well, I I I think the notion of not being dependent I mean you you shouldn't be dependent on on Nvidia. Um you you shouldn't be dependent on one model maker. Um I I I think that that rarely works out well, right? What what you'd like to be as a as a nation or as a big company is you like to have choices. And um I don't know if you need to own all the stack, but you you like choices at each layer of the stack, right? I think a different way to think about what what Alex said is, you know, you want to think about where your advantage is, right? If if you're really you have unique data, be sure you don't give that away, right? Um be sure you have multiple choices in different parts of the stack, but you get the credit for your data. and it doesn't help make somebody else's model better. >> As we look forward, I guess more on the macro lens, the proliferation of AI and everything that we're be able we're able to now create and build and do. What are you excited about on the externalities that come with all of this? >> Okay. I think um that we have a chance for our children or the next generation not only to not die from cancer but to not know anybody who died from cancer. I think that is a a real uh achievable goal in 25 years. Wouldn't that be something? Um, you know, when we think about what AI can do, uh, writing better code is cool and there's a huge market for that, but I I think what what it can do to better humanity is rid us of the number one killer of of adults. And um, I think, you know, that that's when when I think of what we're all doing this for, it's an outcome like that. you know, pancreatic cancer had a huge breakthrough recently. I I think there uh the opportunity for for breakthroughs right now is has never been better and and AI is a an extraordinary tool in in pursuit of of of knocking down um major major human killers, right? I mean, if you think you if you took out cancer and you took out automobile accidents, you're taking out huge numbers of deaths a year. Um, and you say to yourself, well, that's a lot of good that that we did. And I I think, you know, self-driving, humans are terrible drivers. >> Horrible. >> We're horrible drivers. It's not just when we're 16 or 18 or when we're not paying attention or our parents in [snorts] their 80s or, you know, it's it's we we don't pay attention. We're on the phone. We're talking to our wife, we're worried about work. That's even before people drink or do all sorts of things that are obviously bad. But we're just not good drivers and machines can drive better than we can today. And so that's the number one killer of of people, whatever 15 to 40 is car accidents. You take that out through self-driving. You take out a major killer like like cancer going, whoa, that was a pretty good 20-y year run of technology. >> Are you a peptide fan? >> I'm a peptide fan. >> You are? >> Yeah. [laughter] I think uh um not not in the specifics of there is a peptide, but rather in that our opportunity to to advance our knowledge about um the biology of our bodies and and and how to achieve performance and and how to achieve longevity and um we're just beginning. >> Yeah. And we're going to make some big strides, whether it's with peptides or something else or, you know, the next GLP1 inhibitor, whatever. We're going to make giant strides. >> Yeah, it's been really cool to see all the new drug discoveries and new drug disco discovery companies. Brian Armstrong just came out with this company called New Limit. That's one of them. >> Um I think their goal is to eradicate all diseases, >> which is a good one. But >> it's not not right. I mean, even if that's a little hubris, I mean, it wasn't even hubris that was thinkable a decade ago. >> Yeah. >> Right. I mean, that we're now in a realm where, wow, that is sort of crazy big, but not insane, right? I mean, how cool is that? >> Yeah. I mean, well, a lot of people like to talk about the dumerism of AI, but we're I think we're entering a new mix of talking about the actual outcomes with it. And so to your point of what you're talking about, whether it's with AVs or drug discovery, it's it's really good. >> I I think the problem with the doomers is they're only looking at one side of the ledger, right? I think to to look at this with clear eyes, you got to look at both sides of the ledger. You got to say, look, we're going to use a lot of power. It's true. And and AI has some real risks. It's true. And and on the other side of the ledger, here's some things it can do really differently. And here's some things it can do that that take education, right? We we've known for 2,000 years the right way to educate children. And we never do it ever. I mean, we knew that the right way to educate Alexander the Great was to have a tutor. And the smartest tutor, Aristotle was. And that you teach each child differently. And you think about their way of learning. And we never do that. You throw them in a classroom, you teach to some sort of middle level. Um, each child does not get sort of any different teaching for their different way of learning. With AI, we can do that. We can get you tutored that's right for you. And what's more, the tutor can be running in the background saying, "Look, 3% of students make this type of error, and the best way to teach them to overcome this weakness is with this approach." Right? How cool is that? I mean, we've been screwing this up for 2,000 years, and now we can bring it to every child. Um, right? You can put it on the positive side of ledger. Um, and look at clear eyes at both the negative and the positive and see if we're doing right by society. >> So, one of our sponsors is Rex. And I bring this up because our questions around performance, they are all about spending smarter, moving faster, but performance in terms of like for your finances. I asked this question in terms of your personal side of things. Um, I do believe that performance for individuals is kind of who you surround yourself with or who you're inspired by or mentored by. You know, you've had a great run at building companies and have had great success. So, I'm really curious who those people are for you. >> There was there a couple mentors. There's there was a venture capitalist named Pierre Lamont and he's now in his 90s. He he invested in us at uh at Cerebris when he was in the ripe age of 84. >> Oh my gosh. And was on our board and he's forgotten more about making chips than than I'll ever know. Um uh there was a there's a CEO, former CEO named Mark Lesley. He was CEO of Veritas. Um they invented the file system and these were were sort of wise people and uh they had extraordinarily high standards and they um they taught me a lot about being a leader um about demanding a great deal for myself and from others. um they were people who who were exceptional. I think sort of on a day-to-day basis sort of my co-founders we there are five of us. Five is in almost every case too many founders. [laughter] Uh uh they all work we all work together at my last company. Um I've learned an enormous amount from them. um they they they challenge me and and um I'm excited to think with them and that's that's an okay way to go into work every day. >> It's amazing. [snorts] Well, thank you so much, Andrew. It's a pleasure to have time. I know we covered a lot of ground with this conversation. >> It's so many topics. I didn't think we were going to get to peptides. I'm going to be honest. [laughter] >> I just had to ask. >> Well, thank you for having me on your show. I really appreciate it. >> Thank you. >> Huge thank you to the entire Raise team for an incredible event. And thank you to Brex, MongoDB, and Assembly AI for making this trip and series possible. If you enjoyed this conversation, you're going to love the rest of the raise series with Tony Kim from BlackRock, Scott Woo from Cognition, Andrew Feldman from Cerebras, Rodrigo Leang from Salmonova, Michael Hurstston from Lumenum, CJ Desai from MongoDB, and many, many more like our hot takes that we did at a secret location that you can find on X, YouTube, and Instagram. Subscribe to Sorcery on YouTube for more conversations with the people shaping AI and join the free newsletter. You can also do paid at sorcery.vc for weekly insights on AI, robotics, enterprise software, consumer, semiconductors, did I say AI? AI again, and everything that's coming next like funding announcements and all big things in tech. Thank you. Bye.
